title: How the body stores the memory of being held or not held
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

htmlContent:
The first time I understood my body was listening

I was lying on a friend's couch. We weren't talking. She was reading. I was trying to rest. And then she reached over and put her hand on my shoulder. Just her hand. Light. Warm. Present. And I started crying. Not sad crying. Not happy crying. Something else. Something older. My body was speaking a language I didn't know I still knew.

Here's the thing. That hand on my shoulder wasn't just a hand on my shoulder. It was a signal. A signal that traveled through my nervous system and landed somewhere deep. Somewhere that had been waiting for that signal for years. Maybe decades. My body remembered being held. But it also remembered not being held. And it held both memories at the same time.

Think about it. When you were a child, someone held you. Or they didn't. Or they did sometimes. Or they did but with tension in their arms. Or they held you but their mind was somewhere else. Or they never held you at all. Your body recorded every single one of those moments. Not as a story. Not as a memory you can access with your thinking mind. But as a felt sense. A pattern of tension. A way of bracing. A way of collapsing. A way of reaching. A way of withdrawing.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, showed us that the nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. Your body doesn't stop. It doesn't take breaks. It's always asking: Am I safe here? Can I rest here? Can I be seen here? And the answer it finds depends on what it learned in those early moments of being held or not held.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). It gives you a map for understanding why your body responds the way it does. Why your throat tightens when someone reaches for you. Why your chest hollows out when someone leaves the room.

The body's archive of touch

I've worked with people who cannot tolerate being touched. Even a hand on their shoulder makes them flinch. I've worked with people who crave touch so desperately they lose themselves in it. Both are responses to the same question. Both are answers written in the body's tissue.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades studying how animals in the wild process threat. They shake. They tremble. They discharge the energy. And then they go back to grazing. But humans? We hold onto it. We store it. We freeze. And that freezing happens in the muscles, in the fascia, in the way we hold our breath.

When you were held as a child, your nervous system learned something. It learned that closeness meant safety. That another person's body could be a place of rest. That you could let your weight fall into someone else's arms and trust they would catch you. Your muscles learned to soften. Your breath learned to deepen. Your heart learned to slow down.

When you were not held, your nervous system learned something different. It learned that you were alone. That no one was coming. That you had to hold yourself together because no one else would. Your muscles learned to brace. Your shoulders learned to climb up toward your ears. Your jaw learned to clench. Your breath learned to stay shallow.

Wild, right? Your body doesn't forget. It doesn't forgive. It doesn't negotiate. It just keeps the score. Bessel van der Kolk wrote a whole book about this. He called it The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). And it's true. Your body keeps a record of every embrace and every absence. Every time someone pulled you close. Every time someone turned away.

I remember working with a client who had a pattern of freezing when her partner tried to hold her. She wanted to be held. She craved it. But her body would go rigid. Her arms would lock at her sides. She couldn't breathe. Her body was telling a story she couldn't speak. The story was about a mother who held her but only when she was sick. Only when she was still. Only when she didn't move. Her body learned that being held meant being immobilized. That being close meant being trapped.

The nervous system's two doors

Your nervous system has two main settings connection. One says: I can approach. I can reach out. I can trust. The other says: I must withdraw. I must protect. I must survive.

Which door you walk through depends on what your body learned about being held. If you were held consistently, with warmth and presence, your ventral vagal pathway developed. That's the part of your nervous system that allows for social engagement. For eye contact. For soft voice. For safe touch. Your body learned that connection is possible.

If you were not held, or held inconsistently, your sympathetic or dorsal vagal pathways took over. Your body learned that the world is not safe. That people are not safe. That you must either fight for connection or flee from it or freeze and hope it passes.

Stay with me here. This isn't about blame. This isn't about your parents being bad people. Most parents did the best they could with what they had. But your body doesn't care about intentions. It only cares about patterns. And the patterns it learned are the patterns it repeats.

I've had to learn this the hard way. For years, I couldn't let anyone hold me. Not really. I would let people touch me. I would let them put their arms around me. But I never let myself be held. I never let my weight fall. I never let my guard down. My body was still waiting for the other shoe to drop. Still waiting for the warmth to turn cold. Still waiting for the arms to pull away.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). It's not a substitute for human touch. But it can help your body start to release some of the holding patterns that have been locked in place for years.

Relearning what safety feels like

Here's the thing I wish someone had told me earlier. Your body can learn new things. It can update its map. It can change. But it takes time. It takes repetition. It takes the kind of patience that feels impossible when you've been waiting your whole life to feel safe.

The process of healing the body's memory of being held or not held is slow. You can't talk your way out of it. You can't think your way out of it. You have to feel your way through it. You have to let your body have new experiences of being held. Safe experiences. Predictable experiences. Experiences where you can start to let go, a little at a time.

This might mean starting with something small. A weighted blanket. A warm bath. A self-touch practice where you put your hand on your own heart and just breathe. It might mean working with a somatic therapist who understands how to titrate touch. Who knows that too much too fast can retraumatize. Who will let you set the pace.

I worked with a therapist who had me practice reaching out my hand. Just reaching. Not touching. Just the gesture. And then pulling it back. Over and over. My body had to learn that I could reach and not be rejected. That I could extend toward someone and still be safe. That I could choose connection and still have control.

Deb Dana, who works extensively with polyvagal theory, talks about "glimmers." Small moments of safety. A shaft of sunlight. A bird song. A warm cup of tea. These glimmers help your nervous system start to shift toward the ventral vagal state. Toward the state where being held becomes possible.

Honestly, this work is not linear. Some days you'll feel your body open. Some days it will slam shut again. That's not failure. That's the nervous system doing its job. It's protecting you the only way it knows how. The goal isn't to force it open. The goal is to show it, over and over, that it's safe enough to soften.

What your body is still waiting for

I think about this a lot. About what my body is still waiting for. About the holding it never got and still craves. About the holding it got but couldn't trust. About the holding that came with conditions and strings and unspoken demands.

Your body is still waiting too. I can feel it when I write these words. I can feel it when I sit with clients. There's a longing that doesn't go away. A hunger that no amount of self-help can fill. A part of us that just wants to be held. Wants to be safe. Wants to rest.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). It offers exercises that can help you start building that sense of safety from the inside out. Because sometimes you have to be your own anchor before you can trust someone else to hold you.

I don't have a neat ending for this. There's no five-step plan that fixes the body's memory of being held or not held. There's no book or technique or product that replaces what you needed and didn't get. But there is this: the possibility of new experience. The possibility that your body can learn, slowly, that it's okay to let someone in. That it's okay to be held.

Your body remembers everything. But it can also learn new things. It can update its map. It can create new pathways. It can find safety in places it never expected. That's not a guarantee. It's not a promise. It's just a possibility. And sometimes, that's enough to start.
